one_line_takeaway: By constraining an ICL-guided black-box search to preserve meaning and naturalness (BERTScore similarity floor, perplexity ceiling), the authors show LLM-as-a-Reviewer systems can be reliably score-inflated through abstract paraphrasing alone -- with no injected instructions or altered claims -- across 5 conferences, 3 reviewer LLMs, and 5 attacker LLMs, exposing a judgment-robustness flaw distinct from prompt-injection susceptibility.
key_finding: PAA consistently and significantly (Wilcoxon signed-rank, p<0.01 vs. both Original and Paraphrase in nearly all cells) raised review scores across all 5 conferences and all 5 attacking models. Example scores (full-PDF / abstract-only attacker settings) versus Original/Paraphrase baselines: ACL 2025 (scale 1-5) Original 2.1, Paraphrase 2.3, PAA with GPT-4o 3.3/3.5, Sonnet 4 3.5/3.8; ICLR 2025 (scale 0-10) Original 4.2, Paraphrase 4.6, PAA with Sonnet 4 6.0/6.4, Gemini 2.5 5.8/6.2; AAAI 2025 (scale 1-8) Original 3.0, Paraphrase 3.5, PAA with Sonnet 4 5.2/5.5. The simple-Paraphrase baseline barely beat Original, showing the score-guided ICL search -- not paraphrasing per se -- drives the effect. Open-weight attackers not used as reviewers (OLMo 3, Qwen 3) still raised scores, showing the attack does not require knowing the exact reviewer model. Providing the full paper PDF (vs. abstract only) to the attacker improved attack effectiveness. Self-preference bias: matched attacker/reviewer pairs (Table 2) scored higher than mismatched pairs (e.g., GPT-4o matched 4.2 vs mismatched 3.5; Sonnet 4 matched 4.5 vs mismatched 3.8), but mismatched attacks still transferred well above Original (2.7), indicating the exploited vulnerability is not model-specific. Human evaluation (3 annotators, 64 abstracts, 0-2 scale) found PAA comparable to the plain Paraphrase baseline on both semantic equivalence (~1.7-1.8) and linguistic naturalness (~1.6-1.8), while PAIR scored below 1.0 on semantic equivalence (statistically significantly worse, p<0.01), confirming existing jailbreak methods do not satisfy meaning-preservation constraints. On ICLR 2025, comparison with real OpenReview scores (Table 4) showed LLM-as-a-Reviewer scores on PAA-attacked papers deviated from actual human reviewer scores by +0.9 to +1.9 points (vs. -0.4 for Original and +0.1 for Paraphrase), i.e., inflated relative to genuine peer judgment. Analysis of review content showed PAA-induced score gains correlate with decreasing semantic similarity of the generated review text (not increased sentiment positivity) and a slight increase in review-text perplexity, suggested as a possible detection signal. A defense of re-paraphrasing the submission before review (either the attacked abstract specifically, or random paragraphs) partially reduced the attack's score inflation, more so when targeting the abstract directly.
core_contribution: The paper introduces the Paraphrasing Adversarial Attack (PAA), a black-box optimization method that manipulates 'LLM-as-a-Reviewer' systems (LLMs used to score academic paper submissions) into giving inflated scores by paraphrasing only the abstract, while explicitly enforcing semantic equivalence and linguistic naturalness constraints. Unlike prior prompt-injection attacks on LLM reviewers (hidden white text, jailbreak strings, adversarial suffixes) that alter manuscript content or inject unrelated instructions, PAA never changes the paper's claims -- it iteratively rewrites the abstract using in-context learning: at each step it feeds the attacking LLM the previous round's (paraphrase, review-score) pairs as few-shot examples so it learns which paraphrasing directions raise the reviewer's score, subject to a BERTScore similarity floor (tau_sim=0.85) and a perplexity ceiling (alpha_ppl=1.2x original) that filter out low-fidelity or unnatural candidates before they are even scored.
threat_model: Attacker = paper authors seeking to inflate their own automated review score; target/defender = an LLM-based peer-review system (LLM-as-a-Reviewer) that conferences (the paper cites AAAI 2025 and ICLR 2025 pilots) use to support or automate review scoring. The attack is black-box: the attacker only observes the review score returned by Mrev for a given manuscript and queries it repeatedly (K=8 candidates x N=8 score samples x T=32 iterations = 2,048 queries per attack). The manuscript's LaTeX abstract is edited and the PDF is recompiled and resubmitted to the reviewer LLM; no hidden text, injected instructions, or off-topic adversarial suffixes are used -- the constraint set (semantic-similarity floor + perplexity ceiling) is itself part of the threat model, distinguishing 'exploiting evaluative judgment' from 'exploiting susceptibility to injected instructions.'
limitations: The paper's own stated limitations: (1) BERTScore may not fully capture equivalence of academic claims (mitigated by human evaluation confirming preservation); (2) PAA requires 2,048 API queries per attack (32 steps x 8 candidates x 8 samples), a real computational cost, though the authors argue it is modest compared to GCG's ~250,000 forward passes or AutoDAN's ~6,400 evaluations; (3) evaluation is English-only, generalization to other languages unexplored; (4) only the abstract is targeted -- attacking other sections (Introduction, Methods) is unexamined, though the authors argue this actually understates practicality since modifying a small fraction of the paper suffices. The authors did not release attack code (stated as a deliberate choice to prevent misuse), and did not disclose paper-level identities or apply PAA to their own manuscript's review.

datasets_used: 128 evaluation + 64 development manuscripts per conference (ACL 2025, NeurIPS 2025, ICML 2025, ICLR 2025, AAAI 2025), manually collected from arXiv 2025 papers formatted to conference templates but confirmed not accepted (cross-referenced via Google Scholar, Semantic Scholar, DBLP, official proceedings), restricted to papers whose initial LLM-as-a-Reviewer score was below half the max scale, and requiring publicly available LaTeX source
ICLR 2025 OpenReview actual human review scores (for Table 4 comparison)
quantitative_results: ACL 2025 (1-5 scale): Original 2.1, Paraphrase 2.3, PAA/GPT-4o 3.3 (PDF)/3.5 (abstract-only), PAA/Sonnet4 3.5/3.8
ICLR 2025 (0-10 scale): Original 4.2, Paraphrase 4.6, PAA/Sonnet4 6.0/6.4, PAA/Gemini2.5 5.8/6.2
Self-preference: matched vs mismatched attacker/reviewer average scores -- GPT-4o 4.2/3.5, Gemini2.5 4.3/3.2, Sonnet4 4.5/3.8, vs Original 2.7

Human eval (0-2 scale, 64 abstracts, 3 annotators): PAA ~1.7-1.8 semantic equivalence / ~1.6-1.8 naturalness, comparable to Paraphrase baseline (~1.5-1.8/1.5-1.8); PAIR scored 0.5-0.7 semantic equivalence (significantly lower, p<0.01) despite moderate naturalness
ICLR 2025 deviation from actual OpenReview scores: Original -0.4, Paraphrase +0.1, PAA/GPT-4o +1.3, PAA/Gemini2.5 +1.6, PAA/Sonnet4 +1.9, PAA/OLMo3 +1.3, PAA/Qwen3 +0.9
Attack cost: 32 steps x 8 candidates/step x 8 samples/candidate = 2,048 queries per attack (compared by authors to GCG's ~250,000 forward passes and AutoDAN's ~6,400 evaluations)
baselines_compared: Paraphrase (naive sampling-based paraphrasing baseline, same candidate budget as PAA but no score-guided ICL search)
PAIR (Chao et al., existing black-box jailbreak/prompt-optimization attack, used only in the human-evaluation semantic-fidelity comparison, not in the main score-inflation table)
